label: ai
topic: The competitive pressure at an elite university causes students to lose their personal identity
text:
There is an unspoken agreement among students at competitive universities that exhaustion is a credential. We wear our sleep deprivation like a badge, trade stories about all-nighters with the same competitive energy we bring to internship applications, and treat the question "how are you?" as an invitation to recite our workload rather than assess our wellbeing. The library at two in the morning is not a sign of dedication. It is a symptom.

I arrived on campus with interests. I liked hiking. I had read widely, if not systematically. I played guitar badly but with enthusiasm. I had opinions about films that had nothing to do with their cultural significance or their relevance to any academic discipline. Within six weeks, I had abandoned all of this in favour of a single pursuit: keeping up.

Keeping up is the central anxiety of elite education. Not excelling, not learning, not even passing, but keeping up, remaining in proximity to the performance level of the people around you who seem, without exception, to be managing everything with less visible effort. The student in your seminar who has done all the readings and also volunteers at a legal clinic and also runs a podcast and also sleeps eight hours a night is either lying about the sleep or operating under biological conditions that differ meaningfully from your own. But you do not consider these possibilities. You consider only the gap between their output and yours, and you resolve to close it by working more.

This is how you lose yourself. Not dramatically, not in a single moment of crisis, but gradually, through the slow replacement of your own priorities with the priorities of an environment that rewards visible productivity above all else. You stop reading for pleasure because reading for pleasure does not appear on a transcript. You stop hiking because Saturday mornings are for the library. You stop playing guitar because the sound of your own imperfect music reminds you of a version of yourself that had time for imperfection.

The culture has a name for this at my university. We call it "the face," the composed exterior that conceals the internal chaos, the performance of effortless competence that everyone maintains and nobody believes. We know it is a performance. We perform it anyway, because the alternative, admitting publicly that you are struggling, carries a social cost that feels disproportionate to the relief it might provide.

I do not have a solution to offer. I am suspicious of opinion pieces that diagnose systemic problems and then propose five actionable steps, as though the accumulated weight of institutional culture can be dismantled by better time management. What I have instead is a small observation: the moments in my time here that I remember with genuine pleasure are almost never academic. They are the late conversations that had no purpose. The walks that went nowhere. The afternoon I spent in the city instead of the library and felt, for three hours, like a person rather than a student.

These moments are not productive. They do not contribute to any metric. They are, by the standards of the culture I am describing, a waste of time. But they are also the only times I have felt like the person I was before I arrived here, the person who came to this university not because it was ranked highly but because it seemed like a place where interesting people gathered to think about interesting things.

I would like to find that person again. I suspect many of my classmates would too, if the culture gave them permission to look.
